claim: Coding agents need only two foundational things to function effectively: prompt engineering and context engineering
actionable: When debugging agent failures, trace whether the issue stems from unclear prompts (refine instructions) or insufficient context (provide more relevant background), not model capability alone.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Context windows should be capped at ~40% of model capacity (e.g., 100,000 tokens max for 200k models) to maintain model effectiveness
actionable: Monitor token usage during agent runs; if context exceeds 40% of the model's limit, pause and refactor by removing non-critical context to restore performance.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Rules are static guardrails that scale poorly—keeping them under 150-200 lines prevents context bloat; Skills are preferable for scaling due to progressive loading
actionable: Limit static rule files to 150-200 lines; convert larger rulesets into discrete Skills with name/description headers so the agent progressively loads only relevant sections.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Skills use progressive context loading: agent reads only name and description first to decide which skill to use, then loads the body on demand, avoiding one-shot context dumps
actionable: Write skills with clear, specific names and descriptions; place detailed examples and references in separate linked documents so the agent loads them only when needed.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Rules are non-deterministic (agent may not follow them) while Skills are more discoverable by description, making Skills preferable for larger codebases but less guaranteed than statically grounded rules
actionable: Use Rules for critical, non-negotiable standards (e.g., security constraints); use Skills for patterns you want the agent to discover and apply flexibly based on task context.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Always use plan mode before act mode in coding agents—plan mode generates structured reasoning that the agent can then execute more efficiently
actionable: When prompting a coding agent, invoke plan mode first to review the proposed approach, refine it with human feedback, and only then switch to act mode for execution.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: MCPs with 50+ tools overload the agent context window; Abilities group tools by category (e.g., Apex, LWC) so the agent only considers relevant tools per task
actionable: Enable Abilities in your coding agent settings to group MCP tools by domain; this reduces context waste from irrelevant tools and improves inference speed.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Hooks are deterministic custom logic triggered at specific lifecycle points (pre-tool-use, post-tool-use, compact-task) allowing agents to auto-correct outputs via bash scripts
actionable: Create bash-based hooks (e.g., post-tool-use) to auto-format and lint agent-generated code; hook filenames must match lifecycle event names (e.g., `post_tool_use`).
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Sub-agents enable parallelism and solve context overload by spinning up independent workers with dedicated skills; models with better reasoning (e.g., Opus) handle dependencies automatically
actionable: For large tasks (e.g., building account service + LWC + tests), spawn 2-3 sub-agents with specialized skills instead of forcing a single agent through sequential steps.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: If a Skill is not being triggered, the issue is usually a vague skill description—fix the description/title to match task intent, not the skill content itself
actionable: When a skill is ignored, rewrite its name and description to be more specific and aligned with common task prompts; test with evals (example prompts) to verify triggering.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: MCPs will eventually be replaced by Skills as the primary mechanism for exposing tools; this reduces complexity and allows progressive loading vs. dumping all tool descriptions at once
actionable: Start building Skills now for your most-used tools/workflows; avoid deep investments in custom MCP servers as the industry moves toward Skills-based architectures.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Evals (evaluation prompts) allow you to test and iterate on Skill descriptions until they trigger 100% of the time, ensuring determinism through skill discovery testing
actionable: Write 5-10 example prompts that should trigger your Skill; use eval frameworks to verify the Skill is consistently discovered; iterate on the description until all tests pass.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Building codebases for agents is like building factories—initial setup (skills, rules, docs) takes 1-2 hours but pays off immediately for reuse and parallelization
actionable: Invest time upfront in documenting patterns, creating reusable skills, and writing evals; this multiplies efficiency for all future tasks using those skills.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Humans remain essential at every stage: prompt iteration, plan review, skill authoring, test definition, and code review—agents will not eliminate these roles
actionable: Adopt an 'engineer supervisor' mindset: set up agent workflows, validate outputs, refine prompts iteratively rather than expecting fully autonomous code generation.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents

claim: Frontier models (e.g., Opus) dramatically outperform smaller models (Core/Pro) for agentic workflows and dependency resolution; model choice matters significantly
actionable: For critical agentic tasks (multi-step workflows, sub-agents), use the highest-capability model available; reserve smaller models for simple, single-turn generation.
source_title: Engineering Your Coding Agent with Rules, Skills, MCP, Abilities, Hooks & Sub-Agents
